evidence_quotes.quote: realm of digital products for Video Games.

If these Video Games are treated as a service by the developers of the Video Game, it should be clearly stated as such and it should be the customers' right to know when they are expected to lose access to the service
otherwise, as a product, purchase of the product should mean that the customer has personal ownership of their purchased product and have the right to use it and possess it for as long as they wish.

paragraphs.text: I am writing to bring attention to the unfairness and my wish for the further prevention of the removal of digital products by distributors. More specifically this concerns the realm of digital products for Video Games. In many recent years, Video Game publishers have sold Video Games at a real world monetary price to customers, who make their purchase with the expectation of owning what they pay for as per typical rules and standards of basic commerce, who are then, at a later time, unfairly stripped of their access to the product that they have purchased. This can most commonly be attributed to the developers of the Video Game in question to end their active support for said Video Game. Not only can this result in customers purchasing a Video Game, only to have their fairly purchased product be seized by the publisher without even allowing the customer to have a chance to use the product in the first place, but it also aggresses against those who have purchased and used the product as it is their expectation that they possess ownership of that product - which, upon purchasing, is theirs to use and do with as they please. This is an unfair and hostile imbalance of commerce and trade that, until now, has gone unspoken about which has given even more time to Video Game publishers to enact the same discrepancy upon many Video Games over the years. If these Video Games are treated as a service by the developers of the Video Game, it should be clearly stated as such and it should be the customers' right to know when they are expected to lose access to the service - otherwise, as a product, purchase of the product should mean that the customer has personal ownership of their purchased product and have the right to use it and possess it for as long as they wish. In cases such as this, the Video Game that is taken from customers is never released again for free-use, or open-source download, or any other relevant re-issuing of the product that can give paying customers a substitute or replacement for the product that they have been denied access to. Alongside this, there is also an issue upon where digital storefronts retain the right to revoke paying customers; access to Video Games they have paid for which have been sold to them as products. Upon the purchase of a product, physically or digitally; there should be no doubt or insecurity on the part of the consumer on whether they own and/or can continue using their product that they have purchased. This is simply not the case for these digital storefronts that sell Video Games as a product, yet have the ability to remove, revoke or otherwise deny access to paid materials. It is my wish that these issues can be examined and solved within ongoing and future discussions about and around the Digital Fairness Act. Thank you.
